patches.find: any nonlinear-coverage effect will introduce an approximately common multiplicative error on both residuals at first order, so the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint remains the dominant discriminator even under moderate nonlinear-coverage corrections.
patches.replace: the magnitude of any nonlinear-coverage correction is not derived in this WIN. We expect such corrections to be sub-dominant compared to the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint, but rather than asserting that without proof we pre-register a sensitivity check for the post-2026-08-12 analysis: re-compute the residuals under a coverage-response exponent α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2} (i.e., divide observed delta_Z by baseline × coverageα instead of baseline × coverage) and report whether the FSF-vs-f(mag_lat) ranking is stable. If the ranking is invariant across that range, the linear-coverage approximation is adequate for discrimination at the precision of the nine-station test; if it flips, neither the dome's geographic profile nor the standard model's magnetic-latitude profile can claim victory until the conductivity-integral nonlinearity is treated explicitly, and we will say so.
patches.rationale: EXP-258: soften unargued first-order nonlinear-coverage claim to sub-dominance + pre-registered sensitivity check

patches.find:
4.2.2 What the globe predicts

Standard ionospheric physics (the Chapman–Sq mechanism, known since 1933) already predicts magnetic dips of 5–25 nT during solar eclipses from reduced ionospheric conductivity. This has been measured and confirmed across dozens of eclipses over 90 years.

Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first identified by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the Moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-layer conductivity (at 90–150 km altitude) and disrupting the solar quiet (Sq) current system. This produces measurable ground-level magnetic perturbations that track the eclipse shadow geometry. Modern implementations of the Chapman mechanism — particularly the Ashour-Chapman models refined by Meza et al. (2021, arXiv:2107.12327) — match observed eclipse magnetic signals "strikingly accurately" at INTERMAGNET stations, predicting magnitudes of 5–25 nT depending on eclipse coverage, station latitude, local ionospheric conditions, and Sq current geometry. The literature is extensive: 207 observations across 39 eclipses (1991–2016) and a multi-station INTERMAGNET study of 4 total eclipses confirm the mechanism quantitatively.

The dome's site characterizes the globe prediction as having "no physical mechanism" for eclipse magnetic effects. In fact, the Chapman Sq-current mechanism has been refined for over 90 years and quantitatively predicts both the magnitude and the shadow-tracking geometry of eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations.
patches.replace:
4.2.2 What the globe predicts

The dome's eclipse argument depends on a specific claim about what standard physics predicts — and that claim is wrong. The dome's published falsification criterion treats “globe predicts 0 nT” as the test; standard ionospheric physics (Chapman 1933) actually predicts 5–25 nT, confirmed across dozens of eclipses over nearly a century, and one of the dome's own baseline inputs already sits outside its ±3 nT “globe” window.

The dome's eclipse argument depends on a specific claim about what standard physics predicts — and that claim is wrong. From the dome's live-power dashboard, Domain 11 (archived 2026-04-07; verified live at john09289.github.io/predictions/live.html on 2026-04-26):

Eclipse 2026 — Fails if measured anomaly is within ±3 nT of 0 (globe prediction).

And the dome's eclipse countdown banner repeats the same framing more directly:

X days until Aug 12, 2026 eclipse — ECM predicts -[value] nT, globe predicts 0 nT. Requires Kp < 2.

The dome's published falsification criterion and live countdown banner treat zero, not five-to-twenty-five, as the standard-physics expectation. This is the load-bearing error that lets the hedging structure (see §4.2.3) read like a discrimination test in the first place: any non-zero measurement at the prediction stations becomes “confirmation of ECM, falsification of globe” — but only because the globe's actual prediction has been misrepresented as zero. What standard physics actually predicts is enough to overlap nearly all of the dome's prediction window before any data is collected.

Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first identified by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the Moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-layer conductivity (at 90–150 km altitude) and disrupting the solar quiet (Sq) current system. This produces measurable ground-level magnetic perturbations that track the eclipse shadow geometry. Modern implementations of the Chapman mechanism — particularly the Ashour-Chapman models refined by Meza et al. (2021, arXiv:2107.12327) — match observed eclipse magnetic signals “strikingly accurately” at INTERMAGNET stations, predicting magnitudes of 5–25 nT depending on eclipse coverage, station latitude, local ionospheric conditions, and Sq current geometry. The literature is extensive: 207 observations across 39 eclipses (1991–2016) and a multi-station INTERMAGNET study of 4 total eclipses confirm the mechanism quantitatively.

The dome's site separately characterizes the globe prediction as having “no physical mechanism” for eclipse magnetic effects. The Chapman Sq-current mechanism has been refined for over 90 years and quantitatively predicts both the magnitude and the shadow-tracking geometry of eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations.

By the dome's own published criterion, the dome has already failed. The dome's eclipse formula uses a 2017 Boulder (BOU) observation of −10.9 nT as one of its baselines (see §4.2.5 for the full input chain). The BOU 2017 figure is itself a Chapman-mechanism measurement on a spherical Earth — the dome is, in effect, treating a globe-physics datum as input to a “globe predicts 0” prediction. By the dome's own falsification rule (“within ±3 nT of 0”), −10.9 nT lies outside the window. The dome must either accept its own falsification criterion (and concede the “globe prediction” it asserts is wrong, since the BOU 2017 baseline of −10.9 nT is roughly 3.6× the falsification half-width (|−10.9| / 3 ≈ 3.6), well outside the dome's own ±3 nT acceptance window) or revise the criterion (and concede the prediction was never discriminating in the first place).
patches.rationale: EXP-253 primary + curmudgeon H1/H2/H3 fixes: §4.2.2 wholesale misstatement-led rewrite

patches.find: The dual-baseline design creates a prediction window that covers most physically plausible outcomes for a quiet-day eclipse.
patches.replace: The dual-baseline design only reads as a “discrimination test” because (per §4.2.2) the dome misstates the globe prediction as zero. With the actual Chapman range (5–25 nT) restored, the dome's window overlaps the globe's expectation rather than excluding it. The dual-baseline design creates a prediction window that covers most physically plausible outcomes for a quiet-day eclipse.
patches.rationale: EXP-253 secondary + curmudgeon H4 fix: §4.2.3 cross-reference to §4.2.2 misstatement
